input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is a Living Lab used as an infrastructure testbed within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction and Operations (AECO) domain. It is a building equipped with heterogeneous sensors, actuators and control subsystems that span the entire lifecycle from design through operation, making it a representative candidate for digital‑twin deployment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real‑time monitoring of system states, simulation capabilities for what‑if analysis, lifecycle‑encompassing optimization of energy and operational performance, and rich, interactive 3‑D visualization. It enables facility managers and other stakeholders to query the twin, receive predictive analytics, and issue control commands to the physical infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on Eclipse Hono for real‑time data ingestion and exchange with field devices, Eclipse Ditto for digital‑twin state management and synchronization, a model‑driven framework that incorporates BIM models as the static component, and Unreal Engine 5 for high‑fidelity 3D rendering and immersive interaction. Supporting components include a time‑series database for telemetry storage and a micro‑service architecture that orchestrates data flow and service execution.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The framework provides real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronizes the physical building with its digital counterpart, implying an ongoing, sub‑second update loop; however, explicit synchronization frequencies, latency targets, or temporal granularity levels are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture is modular with a central DT hub that hosts a single primary digital twin instance. Components and services are added via standardized extension points, allowing a hierarchical organization of modules, but the document does not describe multiple independent twin instances or decentralized instances.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system is orchestrated as a modular, service‑oriented platform where the DT Framework acts as a central hub coordinating bidirectional data exchange among BIM‑derived models, custom analytics services, and user interfaces. Runtime configuration management enables dynamic adaptation without redeployment, and standardized extension points support integration of additional services, reflecting a component‑based orchestration pattern.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability is addressed through standardized extension points and support for heterogeneous systems, enabling bidirectional data exchange with external BIM tools, analytics services, and enterprise applications. Specific data exchange protocols or standards are not enumerated in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models are derived from BIM models that are enriched with explicit device definitions (sensors, actuators, controllers) and associated control logic. Through model‑driven engineering the framework extracts structured representations, yielding geometric BIM models, behavioral control models, and analytical models for simulation and analysis. Data sources comprise the static BIM geometry and live sensor data streams; data management is performed by automated transformation pipelines that generate deployable Digital Twin artifacts. Model relationships are expressed via a unified semantic foundation linking each physical component to its digital counterpart, enabling continuous synchronization between the physical building and its twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework provides real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronizes physical building sensor readings and live operational data with the digital twin. Data are streamed from the building control system to the DT through a central hub, enabling up‑to‑date BIM‑derived visualizations. The document mentions continuous synchronization and real‑time capabilities but does not specify concrete data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, quality‑assurance processes, preprocessing steps, or integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are issued from the digital twin to physical building devices via the same central hub, allowing Facility Managers to perform real‑time monitoring and control actions. The interaction is described as bidirectional, but the document does not detail command formats, validation/authentication methods, feedback loops, conflict‑resolution strategies, or execution confirmations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The solution adopts a modular architecture with standardized extension points and runtime configuration management to integrate custom services and analytics. Communication between components occurs over the framework’s central hub, but the document does not provide explicit information on the underlying network protocols, topology, bandwidth or latency requirements, reliability/redundancy mechanisms, or security protocols for network communication.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework defines a six‑step life‑cycle for a Digital Twin: (1) device modeling inside the BIM environment where Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators and controllers; (2) semantic enrichment of the BIM model to create a unified representation; (3) model‑driven engineering (MDE) extraction that transforms the enriched BIM into structured DT representations; (4) automated generation of deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual effort; (5) runtime operation where real‑time data exchange synchronises the physical building and its twin and interactive visualisation supports monitoring and control; (6) evolution through modular extension points that allow DT Engineers to add custom services or plugins. Transition between phases is managed by automated transformation pipelines and role‑based governance (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows a model‑driven development paradigm. Building Control Engineers model devices in BIM; the framework applies MDE principles to extract structured representations and automatically generate DT artifacts. A modular architecture with standardized extension points enables DT Engineers to develop and integrate custom services or plugins, allowing the twin to evolve with changing operational requirements. The approach emphasizes minimal manual intervention, but the document does not detail specific quality‑assurance, testing, validation, or version‑control practices.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin provides interactive visualisation that offers immersive interfaces for monitoring system states and controlling building operations. Continuous real‑time data exchange keeps the twin synchronised with the physical asset, enabling Facility Managers to observe live sensor readings and issue control actions. While the document mentions monitoring and control capabilities, it does not specify particular analytics, predictive insights, or confidence measures for recommendations.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework leverages Building Information Modeling (BIM) as the core semantic standard, aligning with BIM’s status as the de‑facto standard in the construction industry (e.g., implicit use of IFC‑based exchange). It adopts a model‑driven engineering approach that enforces consistent artifact generation across the lifecycle. Moreover, the architecture defines standardized extension points to ensure that custom services and analytics can interoperate within the twin ecosystem, providing a systematic interoperability strategy even though specific external standards (e.g., ISO 19650) are not explicitly cited.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately reflects the Living Lab case study described as an infrastructure testbed in the AECO domain. It correctly notes the system includes sensors and actuators, citing the requirement to 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers', and correctly captures the lifecycle scope mentioned in the text.

The extracted services are directly supported by the text. The abstract and introduction explicitly list 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization' and 'rich, interactive visualization'. The specific capabilities for facility managers to 'interacts with the twin' and 'operates the physical infrastructure' are confirmed in Table I.

The extraction correctly identifies the core technologies mentioned in the text: Eclipse Hono, Eclipse Ditto, BIM, and Unreal Engine 5. However, it inaccurately adds 'time-series database' and 'micro-service architecture' as supporting components; these specific technical details are not present in the provided source text, which reduces the score.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the document's emphasis on 'real-time data exchange' and 'continuous synchronization' without specifying exact frequencies. The document states the framework 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and supports 'live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins', but provides no numerical values for latency or update rates. The extraction correctly identifies this gap.

The document describes a 'central hub' architecture ('The DT Framework acts as a central hub') with a 'modular architecture' using 'standardized extension points'. It focuses on a single deployment ('DT of a Living Lab') and does not discuss multiple independent twins or decentralized architectures. The extraction correctly synthesizes these points.

The document clearly outlines a 'modular, service-oriented DT platform' where the framework acts as a 'central hub' coordinating 'bidirectional data exchange'. It explicitly mentions 'Runtime configuration management' (Table III) for dynamic adaptation and 'standardized extension points' for adding services. The extraction captures these orchestration details precisely.

The document addresses integration through 'standardized extension points' and supports 'heterogeneous systems'. It mentions bidirectional communication with external entities like BIM tools and analytics services. However, it 'does not enumerate' specific protocols or standards, which the extraction correctly notes. Supported by quotes: 'standardized extension points', 'bidirectional communication', 'heterogeneous systems'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

The description of data sources and management is supported by quotes: 'systematically extracts relevant asset data from a BIM model and generates runtime interfaces' and 'Real-time data integration... enabling live synchronization'. The mention of 'automated transformation pipelines' aligns with 'model-to-model and model-to-text based transformations'.

The specific details required by the characteristic definition are absent.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text refers to 'sensor configurations' and 'sensor data' within the context of the model-driven framework, but it does not provide specific details about physical sensing components, their types, measurement capabilities, accuracy, or spatial distribution as requested.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source briefly mentions 'validate the model' and the 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' in the abstract, but the provided sections (I-IV) do not elaborate on model fidelity requirements, validation methods, uncertainty quantification, or error detection mechanisms.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately synthesizes the lifecycle phases described in the text. It correctly identifies the sequence: (1) device modeling by 'Building Control Engineers' who 'define sensors, actuators and controllers directly within the BIM environment'; (2) semantic enrichment and (3) MDE extraction where 'the framework applies MDE principles to extract structured representations'; (4) automated generation of 'deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention'; (5) runtime operation where 'Facility Managers' 'monitor system states' via interactive visualization; and (6) evolution via 'standardized extension points' for 'DT Engineers' to 'develop and integrate custom services'.

The description correctly identifies the 'model-driven development paradigm' and the process of modeling in BIM followed by MDE extraction and automatic generation. It accurately notes the 'minimal manual intervention' mentioned in the abstract. Regarding evolution, it correctly points out the 'modular architecture with defined extension points'. It also correctly states that the document does not detail specific quality-assurance, testing, or version-control practices, as the text only briefly mentions 'validating the model' and focuses on automation rather than QA workflows.

The extraction correctly identifies the interactive visualization for 'monitoring and control' and the role of Facility Managers to 'monitor system states' and 'operate the physical infrastructure'. It accurately notes the 'real-time data exchange' for synchronization. It is also correct in observing that while 'predictive analytics' is mentioned as a general DT capability in the introduction, the specific framework details focus on monitoring and control without specifying 'particular analytics, predictive insights, or confidence measures for recommendations' for the generated artifacts.

The description accurately identifies BIM as the core standard and notes the use of MDE for consistency and extension points for interoperability. However, it mentions 'implicit use of IFC-based exchange,' which is not explicitly supported by the provided text snippets (which only mention BIM and 'AECO-specific standards'). It also correctly notes the lack of specific external standards like ISO 19650. Quotes: 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
